headlines.title: Sweat Bees Reversibly Change Color as Ambient Humidity Rises
headlines.framing: A Biology Letters study documents humidity-driven color shifts in fine-striped sweat bees, linking the effect to microscopic exoskeleton swelling.
reported: Science News: Agapostemon subtilior bees shift from blue-green to coppery green as humidity rises.
Science News: 24 museum specimens exposed to sub-10% and 95% humidity for 55 hours each.
Science News: over 1,000 iNaturalist photos cross-referenced with local humidity data.
Science News: color shift suspected to result from exoskeleton layers swelling, reflecting longer wavelengths.

articles.title: Humidity makes these bees turn green
bullets: Iridescent sweat bees (Agapostemon subtilior) shift from blue-green to a coppery green as ambient humidity increases, a reversible color change documented in a study published April 22 in Biology Letters.

Researchers exposed 24 museum bee specimens to humidity levels below 10 percent and up to 95 percent for 55 hours each, and cross-referenced findings with over 1,000 iNaturalist photos of living bees tagged with local humidity data.

Scientists suspect the color shift occurs because humidity causes microscopic layers in the bees' exoskeletons to swell, increasing the spacing between layers and causing them to reflect longer, redder wavelengths of light.

Functional ecologist Madeleine Ostwald of Queen Mary University of London says the phenomenon may be widespread in insects that produce color through structural layering rather than pigments, and warns that removing insects from their environment changes their color.
articles.summary: A study published in Biology Letters reports that fine-striped sweat bees reversibly change color from blue-green to coppery green as humidity rises, based on experiments with museum specimens and citizen-science photographs. Researchers attribute the shift to swelling of microscopic exoskeleton layers that alter how light is reflected. The authors suggest the effect may be a broadly overlooked phenomenon in structurally colored insects.

claims.claim: Iridescent sweat bees shift from blue-green to coppery green as ambient humidity increases, according to a study published April 22 in Biology Letters.
Researchers exposed 24 museum specimens of Agapostemon subtilior to humidity conditions below 10 percent and at 95 percent for 55 hours each.
Over 1,000 photos of living sweat bees from the iNaturalist citizen science app were analyzed alongside local humidity data.
The color change is suspected to result from humidity causing microscopic layers in the exoskeleton to swell, altering light reflection toward longer, redder wavelengths.
Jorge De La Cruz, an undergraduate at UC Santa Barbara, first noticed the color-change effect while curating bee specimens.
Madeleine Ostwald is a functional ecologist at Queen Mary University of London.
Future work with high-powered microscopes may confirm the swelling-layer mechanism behind the color change.
The phenomenon may be widespread among insects that produce color through microscopic structures rather than pigments.
Ostwald argues that studying insect color in natural environments is critical because removal from context changes the color itself.

headlines.title: Tim Cook Steps Down as Apple CEO with Hardware Chief John Ternus as Successor
headlines.framing: Apple CEO Tim Cook will hand the role to hardware engineering chief John Ternus on September 1 after a fifteen-year tenure.
established: Tim Cook will step down as Apple CEO on September 1, 2026, with hardware chief John Ternus named as his successor.
Ternus currently serves as Apple's senior vice president of hardware engineering.
Cook served as Apple CEO for approximately 15 years, succeeding Steve Jobs.
Apple's market capitalization grew from approximately $350 billion to $4 trillion during Cook's tenure.

articles.title: Apple enters post-Cook era chasing its next hit
bullets: Apple announced that CEO Tim Cook will hand over the reins to hardware chief John Ternus, while Cook remains with the company as executive chairman.
Johny Srouji, credited with Apple's chip success, was promoted to the new role of chief hardware officer in the same leadership reshuffle.
Apple has struggled in AI, repeatedly delaying the most ambitious Apple Intelligence features announced in 2024, and struck a deal with Google to use its Gemini models for future capabilities.
Competitors including OpenAI (which acquired Jony Ive's hardware team for $6.5 billion), Meta, and Google are all advancing rival hardware and AI products that Apple has yet to match.
articles.summary: Apple announced that Tim Cook will step down as CEO, handing leadership to hardware chief John Ternus while remaining as executive chairman, ending an era defined by the iPhone's dominance. The transition comes as Apple faces questions about its next major product category, having struggled to deliver on its AI ambitions and ceded ground to rivals in mixed reality and autonomous vehicles. Axios frames the change as a test of whether Apple can return to the kind of innovation associated with the Steve Jobs era.

Apple faces significant AI challenges, having chosen partnerships with OpenAI's ChatGPT and Google's Gemini over developing proprietary AI, drawing criticism for moving slowly in the sector.
Apple was still affected by Trump's tariffs despite efforts to move manufacturing away from China, and Ternus will need to manage relations with the current US administration.
articles.summary: Apple has announced John Ternus, its vice president of hardware, as the successor to longtime CEO Tim Cook. Ternus, described as a hands-on 'product guy,' will inherit a company facing major challenges in artificial intelligence, where Apple has opted for third-party partnerships rather than proprietary development. The BBC's technology correspondent, who met Ternus informally ahead of the announcement, describes him as polished and composed but notes the company faces tests on AI strategy, tariff pressures, and public-facing leadership style.

bullets: Tim Cook will step down as Apple CEO on September 1, handing the role to longtime hardware boss John Ternus.

articles.title: Apple incoming CEO John Ternus faces a defining challenge: Fixing the company's AI strategy
bullets: Tim Cook's 15-year tenure as Apple CEO will end on September 1, with hardware chief John Ternus set to become only the second Apple CEO since Steve Jobs.

Apple has largely avoided the massive AI capital expenditures made by Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, instead relying on Google's Gemini to power key AI features including a delayed Siri upgrade.
In Apple's latest quarter, iPhone revenue surged 23% year-over-year to $85.3 billion, which Cook described as 'simply staggering' demand for iPhone 17 models.

Analysts quoted by CNBC say Ternus's central challenge will be defining a coherent AI strategy, particularly around AI-enabled hardware and services, as consumer interaction with technology rapidly shifts.

articles.title: Higher Ed’s Trust Problem
bullets: Yale's Committee on Trust in Higher Education released a 58-page report calling for reforms to admissions, tuition transparency, grading standards, and intellectual diversity to restore public faith in universities.

The report identifies three main drivers of public distrust—cost, admissions opacity, and campus culture—and notes that 63 percent of registered voters now say a four-year college degree is not worth the cost, up from 47 percent in 2017.

Critics including Tyler Austin Harper argue the report overlooks the collapse of the tenure system, noting that adjuncts and non-tenure-track faculty now constitute a majority of university staff and lack free-speech protections.

Education policy analyst Frederick Hess said that if universities pursue internal reform, they are more likely to regain public trust and political breathing room than if changes are imposed externally by the Trump administration.
articles.summary: Yale's faculty Committee on Trust in Higher Education released a 58-page report last week recommending sweeping changes to admissions, tuition communication, grading, and intellectual diversity as a means of reversing declining public confidence in elite universities. The report comes amid aggressive Trump administration pressure on universities, ongoing campus controversies, and broader national skepticism about the value of a four-year degree. Experts interviewed by The Dispatch offer mixed assessments, with some praising the self-critical effort and others arguing it fails to address structural economic threats to academic free speech or the disruptive potential of artificial intelligence.

claims.claim: Yale's Committee on Trust in Higher Education released a 58-page report recommending reforms to admissions, cost communication, grading, and intellectual diversity.

Starting with the fall 2026 class, Yale students from families earning under $200,000 will pay no tuition, and those under $100,000 will have tuition, room, and board fully covered.
At Harvard, the median grade for a given course is now an A, with 73 percent of courses carrying an A median as of early 2026.

headlines.title: Trump Extends Iran Ceasefire as Talks Stall Over Naval Blockade
headlines.framing: Trump extended the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on April 21 as peace talks in Islamabad collapsed and Iran refused further negotiations.
established: Trump announced an open-ended extension of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on Tuesday, citing Iran's 'fractured' government and a request from Pakistani mediators Field Marshal Asim Munir and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif.
Iran declined to send a delegation to a second round of peace talks in Islamabad, with officials citing U.S. ceasefire violations including the ongoing naval blockade of Iranian ports.
The U.S. Navy seized an Iranian cargo vessel in the Gulf of Oman after it attempted to bypass the naval blockade; Iran condemned the action as maritime piracy and a grave breach of international law.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi declared the U.S. naval blockade an 'act of war' and a violation of the ceasefire, while the blockade remained in effect after the extension was announced.
Trump stated earlier on Tuesday that he did not want to extend the ceasefire and expected to resume bombing Iran, before reversing course hours later and announcing the extension.
The Strait of Hormuz remained closed to commercial shipping, with ship-tracking data showing no vessels transiting the waterway.

articles.title: Trump extends U.S. ceasefire with Iran at Pakistan's request
bullets: President Trump announced an extension of the U.S. ceasefire with Iran on Tuesday, citing Iran's 'fractured' government and a request from Pakistani leaders Field Marshal Asim Munir and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif.
Iran notified Pakistan it would not send a delegation for planned talks on Wednesday, citing alleged U.S. violations of the ceasefire, including the ongoing naval blockade of Iranian ports.

The main U.S. demands center on restoring commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly 20% of the world's crude oil and natural gas passes — and Iran committing to forgo a nuclear weapon.

Israel's strikes in Lebanon have killed more than 2,300 people and displaced over one million, according to Lebanese authorities, while Iran-backed Hezbollah's attacks have killed at least 12 Israeli soldiers and two civilians.
articles.summary: President Trump extended a U.S. ceasefire with Iran hours before it was set to expire, citing a request from Pakistani officials and Iran's internal divisions. Negotiations remain stalled, with Iran declining to send a delegation to planned talks in Islamabad and accusing the U.S. of violating the ceasefire through its continued naval blockade of Iranian ports. Key sticking points include Iran's nuclear program, control of the Strait of Hormuz, the U.S. blockade, and the ongoing Israel-Hezbollah conflict in Lebanon.

bullets: President Trump announced on Truth Social that the U.S. will extend its ceasefire with Iran following a request from Pakistani leaders, hours before the two-week ceasefire was set to expire.

Iran has not confirmed whether it will attend a second round of peace talks in Islamabad, with Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson Esmail Baghaei citing 'contradictory messages' from Washington as the reason for uncertainty.

The U.S. Navy seized an Iranian cargo ship in the Gulf of Oman over the weekend after it attempted to bypass the U.S. naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz, which Iran's UN ambassador condemned as 'a grave breach of international law' and an 'act of aggression marked by the hallmarks of piracy.'
IEA head Fatih Birol stated on Tuesday that the Iran war has resulted in the 'biggest' energy crisis in history, compounded by the effects of Russia's war in Ukraine.
articles.summary: President Trump announced an extension of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on Tuesday, citing a request from Pakistan's army chief and prime minister who are mediating peace talks, hours before the original two-week truce was set to expire. Iran has not confirmed whether it will participate in a second round of negotiations in Islamabad, with Tehran citing contradictory U.S. signals and objecting to the ongoing naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and the seizure of an Iranian vessel. International leaders, including U.K. Prime Minister Starmer, French President Macron, and Chinese President Xi Jinping, have called for a sustainable ceasefire and the full reopening of the Strait, which the IEA's head described as the site of the largest energy crisis in history.

Iran's ongoing chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz, through which about a fifth of global oil production flows, has caused major instability to global energy prices.

articles.title: Trump extends ceasefire as uncertainty over U.S.-Iran peace talks remains
bullets: President Trump announced on Tuesday he is extending the ceasefire with Iran, citing a request from Pakistan's Field Marshal Asim Munir and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, until Iran's leadership can produce 'a unified proposal.'

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent threatened sanctions on any person or vessel facilitating Iranian oil exports, warning that Kharg Island oil storage will be full within days due to the ongoing U.S. naval blockade.

Democratic Rep. Adam Smith, ranking member of the House Armed Services Committee, told CBS News that Trump's characterizations of negotiations being close are 'not even close to true' and said the administration 'still don't have a plan.'

Iran closed the Strait of Hormuz 'until further notice,' ship-tracking data showed no vessels transiting the waterway, and Maersk advised its ships to avoid the strait due to dangerous and unstable conditions.
articles.summary: President Trump extended the ceasefire with Iran on Tuesday after it was set to expire, citing Iran's fractured government and a request from Pakistani leaders, while directing the U.S. military to maintain its naval blockade. Iran has not publicly committed to attending a second round of peace talks in Islamabad, and Iranian officials have continued to accuse the U.S. of ceasefire violations through its blockade and ship seizures. The Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed to commercial traffic, with Maersk and other shipping firms advising vessels to avoid the waterway.

Vice President JD Vance did not travel to Pakistan on Wednesday and remained in Washington for White House policy meetings.
Democratic Rep. Adam Smith told CBS News that Trump's characterizations of negotiations being close are 'not even close to true' and said the administration still lacks a plan.
Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency declared the Strait of Hormuz closed 'until further notice,' and ship-tracking data showed no vessels transiting the waterway.
The U.S. Treasury sanctioned several companies and individuals, including Mahan Air and its chairman, for aiding Iran's development of attack drones or ballistic missiles.
U.S. Central Command reported that since the start of the blockade, U.S. forces directed 28 vessels to turn around or return to port.
Trump's rapid messaging shifts — from saying Iran 'agreed to everything' to threatening total destruction within 48 hours — reflect deep uncertainty about the state of negotiations.
Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf said Iran does not accept 'negotiations under the shadow of threats' and accused Trump of seeking 'a table of surrender.'
Former Mossad chief Yossi Cohen cautioned that no agreement or ceasefire will change Iran's 'fundamental ambitions,' warning Israel not to trust any deal.

articles.title: How Trump's messaging on Iran has shifted since saying they "agreed to everything"
bullets: On Friday April 17, Trump told CBS News that Iran had 'agreed to everything,' including removing its enriched uranium and stopping support for proxy groups like Hezbollah and Hamas, but Iran's foreign ministry quickly denied any agreement to transfer uranium.

By Sunday April 19, Trump escalated his rhetoric on Truth Social and Fox News, warning that Iran's 'whole country is getting blown up' without a deal and threatening to destroy every Iranian power plant and bridge.

On Monday April 20, Trump told Bloomberg it was 'highly unlikely' he would extend the ceasefire if no deal was reached, and contradicted himself by telling the New York Post the U.S. delegation was already en route to Islamabad while a White House official said they had not yet departed.

On Tuesday April 21, Trump told CNBC 'I expect to be bombing' when the ceasefire expires, saying the military is 'raring to go' and that he did not want to extend the ceasefire even if talks were progressing.
articles.summary: Over roughly 96 hours spanning April 17–21, President Trump's public statements on Iran shifted from claiming Tehran had 'agreed to everything' to threatening to bomb Iranian power plants and bridges if a deal is not reached before the ceasefire expires. Iran's foreign ministry directly contradicted Trump's Friday claim that Tehran had agreed to transfer enriched uranium, calling such a transfer impossible. CBS News documents the timeline of contradictory statements, which also included conflicting accounts from the White House about the status of the U.S. diplomatic delegation traveling to Islamabad.

claims.claim: Trump told CBS News on April 17 that Iran had 'agreed to everything,' including working with the U.S. to remove its enriched uranium.

Trump posted on Truth Social that Iran firing into the Strait of Hormuz constituted a 'Total Violation of our Ceasefire Agreement' and threatened to destroy every Iranian power plant and bridge.
Trump told Bloomberg on April 20 that it is 'highly unlikely' he would extend the ceasefire if no deal is reached before it expires.
Trump told CNBC on April 21 that 'I expect to be bombing' when the ceasefire ends, adding that the military is 'raring to go.'
Trump told the New York Post the U.S. delegation was already traveling to Islamabad, while a White House official said the delegation had not yet departed.
The conflicting statements between Trump and Iran raised questions about how much Tehran had actually agreed to, and whether the president's comments were more hopeful than facts warranted.
Trump's rapid messaging shifts suggest an uncertain and potentially unstable diplomatic negotiating posture ahead of the ceasefire expiration.
Iran said on Monday it had agreed to return to peace talks in Islamabad.

articles.title: Trump and Iran trade conflicting messages over prospect of talks
bullets: Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman said Monday that Tehran has 'no plans for the next round of negotiation' with the U.S., even as President Trump dispatched a senior delegation including Vice President JD Vance, Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner to Pakistan for talks.

The U.S. Navy destroyer USS Spruance fired on and seized the Iranian-flagged cargo vessel Touska in the Gulf of Oman after it attempted to bypass the U.S. blockade, which Iran's military called 'maritime piracy' and vowed to respond to.

Brent crude oil climbed more than 5% to approximately $95.36–$95.62 per barrel as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed following Iran's reversal of its decision to reopen the waterway, while U.S. stocks declined from record highs.

An IRGC aerospace commander claimed Iran has replenished its drone and missile arsenal faster during the two-week ceasefire than before the war began, directly contradicting Israeli Prime Minister Netanyahu's earlier claim that strikes had destroyed Iran's ballistic missile production capability.
articles.summary: Diplomatic efforts to extend or replace a two-week U.S.-Iran ceasefire—set to expire Wednesday—appeared near collapse Monday, as Iran's Foreign Ministry said Tehran had no plans to attend a second round of talks in Pakistan while the U.S. pressed ahead with its naval blockade and seized an Iranian-flagged vessel. President Trump sent a senior delegation including Vice President Vance to Islamabad and issued renewed threats to strike Iranian power plants and bridges if no deal is reached, while simultaneously stating he was 'under no pressure whatsoever' to conclude an agreement. Oil prices surged more than 5% and U.S. stocks fell as the Strait of Hormuz remained closed, with the Lebanese front also active following Hezbollah's first claimed attack since a separate 10-day Israel-Lebanon ceasefire took effect.

Lebanon says at least 2,387 people have been killed and 7,602 wounded by Israeli attacks since the war with Hezbollah erupted six weeks ago.
The UAE said it dismantled an Iran-linked extremist cell of at least 27 men who were planning terrorist and sabotage operations inside the country.

articles.title: Trump extends Iran ceasefire, citing "fractured" Iranian government
bullets: President Trump announced an open-ended extension of the U.S.-Iran ceasefire on Tuesday, hours before it was set to expire, citing Iran's 'fractured' government as justification for the pause.

Trump said the decision came at the request of Pakistani mediators — Field Marshal Asim Munir and Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif — and directed the U.S. military to maintain its naval blockade on Iranian ports.

Iran's supreme leader Mojtaba Khamenei has not yet responded to the latest proposal, with negotiators reportedly waiting for his directive, according to a regional source and an Israeli source familiar with the talks.

Iran's internal leadership is divided, with civilian officials including Foreign Minister Araghchi favoring continued talks while IRGC commander Gen. Ahmad Vahidi and deputies opposed negotiations as long as the blockade continues.
articles.summary: President Trump extended the U.S.-Iran ceasefire indefinitely on Tuesday, hours before it was due to expire, citing Iran's fractured leadership and a request from Pakistani mediators. The extension came despite Trump's own earlier statement that he did not want to prolong the ceasefire, and followed Iran's refusal to participate in a new round of peace talks that had been set to involve Vice President Vance in Islamabad. Iranian officials publicly dismissed the extension as meaningless while Iranian armed forces declared they were fully prepared for a potential U.S. strike.

IRGC commander Gen. Ahmad Vahidi and his deputies refused to offer concessions and opposed negotiations as long as the naval blockade continues, according to a U.S. official and a regional source.

The U.S. seizure of an Iranian cargo ship in the Arabian Sea deepened the internal rift within Iranian leadership, with IRGC commanders accusing negotiators of weakness.
Trump directed the U.S. military to maintain the naval blockade on Iran's ports and remain ready to resume the war if needed.
Extending the ceasefire without a firm deadline risks undercutting Trump's leverage, both the pressure of a ticking deadline and the credibility of his military threat.

articles.title: Trump announces Iran ceasefire extension but says blockade remains

The US naval blockade on Iranian ports remains in place despite the ceasefire extension, with Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi calling the blockade an 'act of war' and a violation of the truce.

Iran did not immediately respond to Trump's announcement, with Tasnim news agency stating Tehran's position would be 'officially announced later,' leaving uncertainty over whether Iran will participate in second-round talks in Islamabad.

Al Jazeera correspondent Ali Hashem reported from Tehran that Trump's claim of a 'fractured' Iranian leadership is likely a 'misconception,' noting that new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei heads a team that has worked together for 15 years.

articles.title: Trump extends ceasefire in Iran, citing 'seriously fractured' Iranian government
bullets: President Trump extended the U.S.-Iran ceasefire beyond its Wednesday expiration, citing Iran's government as 'seriously fractured' and conditioning resumption of talks on Tehran submitting a 'unified proposal.'

The extension came after a planned trip by Vice President JD Vance to Pakistan for a second round of peace talks was put on hold, and Iranian negotiators informed U.S. counterparts through a Pakistani intermediary that they would not attend further negotiations.

An advisor to the speaker of Iran's parliament characterized Trump's ceasefire extension as 'a ploy to buy time' for a surprise strike and warned that the U.S. Navy blockade of Iranian ports 'must be met with a military response.'

The Strait of Hormuz, a critical global oil transit route that Iran closed at the start of the war, remains a central point of contention; Trump has conditioned the ceasefire on its full reopening, but traffic through the passage remained minimal.
articles.summary: President Trump announced Tuesday that he is extending a two-week U.S.-Iran ceasefire, citing the 'seriously fractured' state of Iran's government and the request of Pakistani leaders, and conditioning further progress on Tehran presenting a unified negotiating proposal. The extension follows a breakdown in planned peace talks in Pakistan, with Iranian officials declining to attend a second round and accusing the U.S. of violating a 10-point ceasefire framework. Iranian parliamentary advisors rejected the extension as a pretext for a planned strike and called the ongoing U.S. Navy blockade of Iranian ports an act of war.

A second round of peace negotiations between the U.S. and Iran is underway.

articles.title: ‘New cards on the battlefield’: U.S., Iran ratchet up rhetoric with peace talks in limbo
bullets: Iran's parliamentary speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf warned that Iran has 'prepared to reveal new cards on the battlefield' amid a ceasefire nearing expiry, while accusing the U.S. of violating the truce and seeking a 'table of surrender.'
Trump stated the ceasefire expires 'Wednesday evening Washington time,' said he is unlikely to extend it, and threatened that 'lots of bombs [will] start going off' if no deal is reached.

A first round of peace talks in Islamabad led by Vice President JD Vance, Steve Witkoff, and Jared Kushner ended April 12 with no resolution, and a second round is being planned though no Iranian delegation had yet departed for Islamabad as of Tuesday.

The U.S. Navy fired on and seized an Iran-flagged cargo ship attempting to bypass the blockade of Iranian ports, marking the first significant naval encounter since the blockade began, as Iran also fired on two ships attempting passage through the Strait of Hormuz.
articles.summary: The U.S. and Iran exchanged escalating rhetoric ahead of a ceasefire deadline, with Iran's parliamentary speaker claiming new battlefield leverage and Trump threatening renewed military strikes if peace talks fail. A second round of negotiations in Islamabad was being organized, though no Iranian delegation had confirmed departure as of Tuesday, following an inconclusive first round on April 12. Naval clashes in the Strait of Hormuz and mutual accusations of ceasefire violations have further complicated prospects for an agreement.

headlines.title: Mozilla Firefox 150 Patches 271 Bugs Found by Anthropic AI Model
headlines.framing: Mozilla patched 271 Firefox 150 vulnerabilities pre-release after Anthropic's Mythos Preview AI model identified them through source code analysis.

Wired: Holley warns all software needs AI-assisted vulnerability overhaul.

articles.title: Mozilla Used Anthropic’s Mythos to Find and Fix 271 Bugs in Firefox
bullets: Mozilla's Firefox 150 release includes fixes for 271 vulnerabilities identified using early access to Anthropic's Mythos Preview AI model.

Firefox CTO Bobby Holley stated that AI tools like Mythos Preview can now cover 'the full space of vulnerability-inducing bugs,' a capability previously requiring millions of dollars and human expert analysis.

Holley warned that every piece of software will need to undergo an AI-assisted vulnerability overhaul, noting that engineering leaders at large companies plan to dedicate thousands of engineers to the effort over the next six months.

Mozilla CTO Raffi Krikorian cautioned in a New York Times essay that well-resourced organizations will receive advanced AI security capabilities first, while smaller open source projects risk being left vulnerable.
articles.summary: Mozilla announced that its Firefox 150 release patches 271 bugs discovered through early access to Anthropic's Mythos Preview AI model, marking a notable use of emerging AI vulnerability-hunting tools in a major open source browser. Firefox CTO Bobby Holley described the development as a transformative but finite transition period during which all software must be audited for latent bugs now made discoverable by AI. Mozilla's leadership also raised concerns that smaller open source projects lacking resources may be disproportionately exposed as these capabilities spread to attackers.

headlines.title: Trump Fed Nominee Kevin Warsh Faces Contentious Senate Confirmation Hearing
headlines.framing: Kevin Warsh, Trump's nominee for Federal Reserve chair, testified Tuesday before the Senate Banking Committee on independence, rates, and financial disclosures.
established: Warsh appeared before the Senate Banking Committee on Tuesday for his confirmation hearing as Trump's nominee to replace outgoing Fed Chair Jerome Powell.
Warsh pledged the Fed's monetary policy independence as 'essential' and denied that Trump ever asked him to commit to any specific interest rate decision.
Sen. Elizabeth Warren labeled Warsh Trump's 'sock puppet,' accusing him of shifting from a hawkish to a dovish rate stance to align with Trump.
Sen. Thom Tillis (R-NC) threatened to block Warsh's confirmation until the DOJ investigation into Powell and the Fed over headquarters renovation cost overruns is resolved.
Warsh disclosed financial holdings exceeding $100 million and agreed to divest certain assets to comply with Fed ethics rules if confirmed.
Warsh argued AI-driven productivity gains would allow the Fed to lower interest rates while keeping inflation in check, a shift from his previously hawkish reputation.

articles.title: Here are 3 takeaways as Trump's pick to lead the Fed faces a confirmation fight
bullets: Kevin Warsh, President Trump's nominee to chair the Federal Reserve, faced a Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing on Tuesday amid opposition from at least one Republican senator.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-NC) has pledged to block a vote on Warsh until the Justice Department drops its criminal investigation into the Fed and current chair Jerome Powell over alleged cost overruns on the Fed's headquarters renovation.

Warsh, previously known as a 'hawkish' policymaker, argued at the hearing that productivity gains from artificial intelligence could give the Fed more leeway to lower interest rates, drawing accusations from Sen. Elizabeth Warren that he is aligning himself with Trump's political agenda.

Warsh stated he had made no promises to Trump to lower rates and also called for a narrower Fed mandate, arguing the central bank should avoid wading into political areas such as climate change or inclusion.
articles.summary: Kevin Warsh, President Trump's nominee for Federal Reserve chair, faced a Senate Banking Committee confirmation hearing on Tuesday, where his path to confirmation was complicated by Sen. Thom Tillis's threat to block a vote until a DOJ investigation into the Fed is dropped. Warsh was also pressed on whether his recent shift toward supporting lower interest rates signals political deference to Trump, a charge he denied. If confirmed, Warsh has indicated he would seek to narrow the Fed's role beyond its core mandates of price stability and maximum employment.

articles.title: Ransomware negotiator pleads guilty to helping ransomware gang
bullets: Angelo Martino, a former ransomware negotiator at cybersecurity firm DigitalMint, pleaded guilty to extortion for feeding confidential victim information—including insurance policy limits and negotiation strategies—to the ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware gang.

Martino admitted to helping co-conspirators Kevin Tyler Martin and Ryan Clifford Goldberg deploy ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware against U.S. victims for six months in 2023, collectively earning more than $1.2 million from one victim alone.

He faces up to 20 years in prison, and authorities have already seized $10 million in assets from him; he is the third ransomware negotiator in the past year to face criminal charges for the same double-agent scheme.

Assistant Attorney General A. Tysen Duva stated that Martino 'betrayed' his clients and harmed 'the cyber incident response industry itself,' while DigitalMint said it had no knowledge of his actions and fired the implicated employees.
articles.summary: Angelo Martino, a former negotiator at cybersecurity firm DigitalMint, pleaded guilty Monday to extortion charges after the U.S. Justice Department announced he had secretly assisted the ALPHV/BlackCat ransomware gang while purportedly working on behalf of victims. Martino admitted to sharing victims' insurance limits and negotiation strategies with the gang and helping to deploy its ransomware against multiple U.S. targets over a six-month period in 2023, netting over $1.2 million from a single victim. He is the third ransomware negotiator within the past year to face prosecution for the same type of scheme and now faces up to 20 years in prison.

Martino faces a maximum penalty of 20 years in prison and is scheduled for sentencing on July 9, 2026.
Martino's insider betrayal harmed not only individual victims but also the broader cyber incident response industry, according to the DOJ.
The case illustrates a systemic vulnerability in the ransomware response industry, where trusted intermediaries can exploit their access to victim information.

articles.title: Former DigitalMint ransomware negotiator pleads guilty to extortion scheme
articles.relReason: Article cites a primary plea agreement document (linked for download), named DOJ and FBI officials with direct quotes, and specific financial figures from court records, placing it above the source's typical reliance on secondary reporting.
bullets: Angelo John Martino III, a former ransomware negotiator for DigitalMint, pleaded guilty to conspiring with BlackCat/ALPHV affiliates to extort the same U.S. companies he was hired to represent in negotiations.
Martino shared victims' internal negotiating positions and insurance policy limits with co-conspirators, helping extract a combined $75.3 million in ransom payments across ten victim organizations.

Two co-conspirators — Kevin Tyler Martin, another former DigitalMint negotiator, and Ryan Clifford Goldberg, a former Sygnia incident response manager — previously pleaded guilty in December and are scheduled for sentencing April 30.

Martino faces up to 20 years in federal prison and authorities have seized $10 million in assets including vehicles, a luxury fishing boat, and two Florida properties; sentencing is scheduled for July 9.
articles.summary: Angelo John Martino III, a South Florida man and former ransomware negotiator for DigitalMint, pleaded guilty Monday to conspiring with BlackCat/ALPHV ransomware affiliates to extort the same U.S. companies he was simultaneously representing as a negotiator in 2023. Martino leveraged confidential information about victims' insurance limits and negotiating positions to help co-conspirators maximize ransom payments, which collectively totaled $75.3 million across ten organizations. He faces up to 20 years in prison at a July 9 sentencing, and authorities have seized $10 million in assets tied to the scheme.

claims.claim: Angelo John Martino III pleaded guilty to conspiring with BlackCat/ALPHV ransomware affiliates to extort U.S. companies he was representing as a ransomware negotiator for DigitalMint.
Martino shared victims' internal negotiating positions and insurance policy limits with co-conspirators to maximize ransom payments.

Prosecutors said Martino helped accomplices extort a combined $75.3 million in ransom payments, including a nearly $26.8 million payment from an unnamed nonprofit and a nearly $25.7 million payment from an unnamed financial services company.
DigitalMint fired Martino the day after the Justice Department informed the company of its investigation in April 2025, and DigitalMint is not accused of knowledge or involvement in the crimes.
Co-conspirators Kevin Tyler Martin and Ryan Clifford Goldberg pleaded guilty in December and are scheduled for sentencing April 30.
Authorities seized $10 million in assets and cryptocurrency wallets controlled by Martino, including vehicles, a food truck, a 29-foot luxury fishing boat, and two Florida properties.

ALPHV/BlackCat claimed responsibility for the February 2024 attack on Change Healthcare, which paid a $22 million ransom and became the largest health care data breach on record, compromising data on about 190 million people.
The case represents an extreme but rare example of the dark underbelly of ransomware negotiation, where backchannel negotiations remain largely unscrutinized and can go awry.
Martino's actions harmed not only individual victims but also the broader cyber incident response industry, according to DOJ Assistant Attorney General A. Tysen Duva.
Martino's ability to play both sides of ransomware negotiations by exploiting his trusted negotiator role illustrates systemic vulnerability in the unregulated ransomware response industry.

headlines.title: Two CIA Officials and Two Mexican Officers Die in Chihuahua Crash After Drug Raid
headlines.framing: Two CIA employees and two Mexican officials died in a Chihuahua car crash after a drug lab operation, prompting Sheinbaum to launch a federal investigation.
established: Two CIA employees and two Mexican state investigators died when their vehicle veered off a mountain road into a ravine and exploded in Chihuahua state, Mexico, on Sunday.
The crash occurred as a convoy returned from an operation destroying a clandestine synthetic drug laboratory in the municipality of Morelos in the Sierra de Chihuahua.
U.S. Ambassador Ronald Johnson initially identified the dead Americans only as U.S. Embassy personnel; U.S. officials subsequently confirmed to multiple news outlets that they were CIA employees.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said her government's federal security cabinet was not informed of the operation and ordered a federal investigation into potential national security law violations.
Sheinbaum stated that Mexican constitutional law requires state governments to obtain federal authorization before collaborating with foreign officials.
Sheinbaum said Mexico engages only in information-sharing with the U.S. and that there are no joint land or air operations on Mexican soil.
reported: BBC: Prior Reuters probe found CIA ran covert ops in Mexico with government approval.
Intercept: Northern Command established anti-cartel task force January 2026 on Trump's order.
Time, CBS: Analysts suggest Sheinbaum response may serve as USMCA negotiation leverage.

disputed.claim: What the U.S. officials were doing in Chihuahua at the time of the crash

positions.who: Chihuahua Attorney General César Jáuregui Moreno
positions.asserts: The Americans were drone-training instructors who had completed a separate course and joined the convoy only for transport to the airport, not as participants in the drug lab operation.

positions.asserts: The two Americans were CIA operatives conducting or directly supporting a covert counter-narcotics operation at the time of their deaths.

articles.title: Mexico’s President Calls For Investigation After CIA Members Killed in Cartel Operation
bullets: Two CIA officials and two Mexican state investigators died in a car crash in Chihuahua while returning from a drug raid in the municipality of Morelos on Sunday.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said her government was unaware of direct collaboration between Chihuahua state and U.S. Embassy personnel, and launched an investigation into what the officials were doing and which agency they worked for.

U.S. Ambassador Ronald Johnson initially described the dead as 'two members of staff from the United States Embassy,' but U.S. officials later identified them to the New York Times and Washington Post as CIA members.

Analysts from the Wilson Center and Brookings Institution suggest Sheinbaum's public response is largely driven by domestic political considerations and may serve as a bargaining chip ahead of the July 2026 USMCA review.
articles.summary: Two CIA officials and two Mexican state investigators were killed in a car crash in the northern state of Chihuahua following a counter-narcotics operation, Mexican and U.S. officials confirmed. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum called for an investigation, stating her federal government had not authorized collaboration between U.S. personnel and Chihuahua state authorities. Analysts cited by Time characterize Sheinbaum's public response as driven primarily by domestic political pressures and potential leverage in upcoming USMCA trade negotiations.

claims.claim: Two U.S. CIA officials and two Mexican AEI investigators died in a car crash in Chihuahua while returning from a drug raid in Morelos on Sunday.
U.S. Ambassador Ronald Johnson initially identified the dead only as 'two members of staff from the United States Embassy.'
U.S. officials later identified the dead to the New York Times and Washington Post as CIA members.
Sheinbaum said her government was 'unaware of any direct collaboration between the state of Chihuahua and personnel from the U.S. Embassy in Mexico' and launched an investigation.
Chihuahua Attorney General César Jáuregui said the car 'appears to have skidded at some point and fallen into a ravine, exploding.'
CIA Director John Ratcliffe allotted new resources for counter-cartel work in the U.S.-Mexico border region in early 2025.
Wilson Center fellow Duncan Wood characterized Sheinbaum's response as largely 'political theater' aimed at her domestic Morena party base rather than at Trump.
Brookings Institution fellow Vanda Felbab-Brown suggested Sheinbaum may be using the CIA officials' involvement as a bargaining chip in USMCA negotiations ahead of the July 2026 review.

Sheinbaum's public pushback reflects a broader pattern in which Mexican leaders must perform concern over U.S. operatives for nationalist domestic audiences, even when they know such operations occur regularly.
The Mexican government is running out of concessions it can offer the U.S. on security cooperation, according to Felbab-Brown.

articles.title: U.S. Personnel Who Died in Mexico Were Working for the CIA, Sources Say
bullets: Two U.S. officials who died in a vehicular crash in Mexico's Sierra de Chihuahua on Sunday were CIA operatives conducting covert counter-cartel operations, according to two anonymous sources who spoke to The Intercept.

The Americans died alongside two Mexican officials, including the director of the Chihuahua State Investigation Agency, after their convoy departed from a raid on a synthetic drug lab and drove off a ravine.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said she was unaware of 'any direct work between Chihuahua state and personnel from the U.S. embassy,' and the State Department declined to provide information on the Americans' activities.

U.S. Northern Command established Joint Interagency Task Force-Counter Cartel in January on Trump's order, and Acting Assistant Secretary Joseph Humire confirmed to the House Armed Services Committee that the U.S. would be 'moving to a lot more terrestrial strikes' across Latin America.
articles.summary: Two U.S. government personnel killed in a vehicle crash in Mexico's Sierra de Chihuahua on Sunday were CIA operatives running covert counter-cartel operations, two anonymous sources told The Intercept. The Americans died after a convoy departed from a raid on a synthetic drug lab, alongside Mexican officials including a state police director; the U.S. Ambassador publicly identified them only as embassy staff, and the CIA declined to comment. The deaths come amid a broader U.S. military and intelligence expansion across Latin America under the Trump administration's counter-cartel campaign.

claims.claim: Two U.S. officials who died in Mexico on Sunday were CIA operatives, according to two anonymous sources who spoke to The Intercept.
The Americans died in a vehicular crash in the Sierra de Chihuahua after their convoy departed from a raid on a synthetic drug lab.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said she was unaware of any direct work between Chihuahua state and U.S. embassy personnel.
The CIA declined to comment on the deaths, with a spokesperson noting only 'CIA declined to comment' by email.
U.S. Northern Command established Joint Interagency Task Force-Counter Cartel in January 2026 on Trump's order to coordinate intelligence against cartel networks.
Acting Assistant Secretary Joseph Humire confirmed to the House Armed Services Committee that the U.S. would be moving to more terrestrial strikes across Latin America.
The U.S. has conducted 53 attacks on drug boats since September 2025, killing more than 180 civilians, according to The Intercept's characterization of Operation Southern Spear.
The CIA's covert operations in Mexico, conducted without Mexican federal government knowledge, represent an expansion of U.S. unilateral action that undermines bilateral sovereignty agreements.
The deaths of CIA operatives in Mexico signal that Trump's counter-cartel strategy is escalating from intelligence support toward direct action on Mexican soil.

Mexican authorities' shifting public accounts — initially saying the Americans died following the raid, then claiming they were only conducting drone training — suggest efforts to obscure the nature of U.S. involvement.

articles.title: Mexico investigating after CIA employees die in crash following drug lab raid
bullets: Two CIA employees were killed in a car crash in Mexico's Chihuahua state while returning from an operation to destroy a clandestine drug lab, along with two Mexican officers.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said federal prosecutors are investigating potential national security violations, stating her government was not informed of the operation and that state governments must obtain federal authorization to collaborate with foreign entities.

The Chihuahua attorney general described the crash as occurring when a truck 'appears to have skidded and fell into a ravine, exploding' while driving through rugged mountain terrain at night; the drug lab site was called 'one of the largest sites found in the country where chemical drugs were produced.'

The incident has reignited debate over U.S. involvement in Mexican security operations at a sensitive moment, coinciding with USMCA renegotiation talks and the Trump administration's announcement of visa restrictions on Cartel de Sinaloa family members.
articles.summary: Two CIA employees and two Mexican law enforcement officers died in a car crash in Chihuahua state, Mexico, following a joint operation to destroy a clandestine drug laboratory. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum announced a federal investigation into potential national security law violations, saying her government had not been informed of the operation and that any unauthorized collaboration between state and foreign officials would breach Mexican law. The incident has heightened tensions in U.S.-Mexico relations, which are already strained by the Trump administration's pressure on cartels and ongoing trade negotiations.

claims.claim: Two CIA employees were killed in a car crash in Chihuahua state, Mexico, following a drug lab raid operation.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said federal prosecutors are investigating potential violations of national security related to the incident.
Sheinbaum stated her government's security cabinet was not informed of the operation and that it was a decision by the Chihuahua state government.

The Chihuahua attorney general initially described the two Americans as U.S. Embassy 'instructors' doing routine training work, before later clarifying there 'were no U.S. agents in the operation to secure the narco-lab.'
The drug lab site in Morelos, Chihuahua was described by the Chihuahua attorney general as 'one of the largest sites found in the country where chemical drugs were produced.'
Contradictory statements from Sheinbaum, local officials, and the U.S. Embassy have fueled speculation and confusion about the nature of U.S. involvement.
The Trump administration announced visa restrictions on family members of the Cartel de Sinaloa the same day USMCA renegotiation talks were scheduled to begin.
The incident comes at a pivotal moment in U.S.-Mexico relations, with the Trump administration applying escalating pressure over cartel activity.
Sheinbaum has insisted that there are no joint land or air operations in Mexico with the U.S., only information-sharing within a legal framework.
The presence of U.S. officials on Mexican territory has been an ongoing subject of debate, intensified by Trump's military actions in Venezuela and Iran.

articles.title: Role of US officials killed in crash in Mexico under scrutiny
bullets: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum has ordered an investigation into two US officials, reportedly CIA employees, who died in a car crash in Chihuahua state during a counter-narcotics operation.

The four occupants of the vehicle — two US nationals and two Mexican officials — died when their car skidded off a road and fell into a ravine, which then exploded, according to Chihuahua state authorities.

Sheinbaum said neither she nor senior federal security officials had been informed of any joint US-Mexican operation, emphasizing that foreign officials may only operate in Mexico with prior federal-level clearance.

A 2025 Reuters investigation had previously found that the CIA had been running covert operations in Mexico for years, working closely with special narco-hunting units inside the Mexican military with the Mexican government's approval.
articles.summary: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum has launched an investigation into two US nationals, later confirmed by CBS and other outlets as CIA personnel, who died alongside two Mexican officials in a car crash in Chihuahua state on Sunday following a drug-lab destruction operation. Sheinbaum stated that her government had not been informed of any joint US-Mexican operation in the area, and requested information from both the US embassy and Chihuahua state authorities to assess whether national security law was breached. The incident adds tension to the ongoing sovereignty dispute between Mexico and the United States over counter-narcotics operations on Mexican soil.

claims.claim: Two US nationals, reportedly CIA employees, died in a car crash in Chihuahua state, Mexico, alongside two Mexican officials on Sunday.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum ordered an investigation into the role the US officials played in a counter-narcotics operation.
Sheinbaum said her government and senior federal security officials had no prior knowledge of any joint US-Mexican operation in Chihuahua.
US Ambassador Ronald Johnson described the two Americans as 'US embassy personnel'.

Chihuahua State Attorney-General César Jáuregui said the US officials were 'instructor officers' engaged in 'basic training work' and that the crash occurred as they returned from an operation destroying clandestine synthetic drug labs.
Sheinbaum stressed that while Mexico cooperates with the US on intelligence sharing, 'there are no joint operations on land or in the air'.
A September 2025 Reuters investigation found the CIA had been running covert operations in Mexico for years to track down drug traffickers, working closely with special Mexican military units.

The apparent discrepancy between the Chihuahua attorney-general's account of the officials' role and Sheinbaum's denial of federal knowledge suggests a breakdown in coordination between Mexican state and federal authorities.
The incident is likely to intensify the existing tension between Mexico and the US over sovereignty and counter-narcotics cooperation.

articles.title: Two CIA agents reportedly killed in car crash in Mexican state of Chihuahua
bullets: Two individuals reportedly from the CIA were killed when their car veered off a road and crashed into a ravine in the Mexican state of Chihuahua early on Sunday.

Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum announced an investigation by the Attorney General's Office to determine whether Mexican constitutional or national security law was violated, emphasizing that foreign agents require federal authorization to operate in Mexico.

Chihuahua state authorities have contradicted reports of CIA involvement, claiming the US officials were merely being driven to catch a flight and were in the state as drone-handling instructors, not participants in the drug operation.

The Washington Post, citing anonymous officials, initially reported the agents were engaged in a counternarcotics operation, while US Ambassador Ronald Johnson referred to the deceased only as 'embassy personnel.'
articles.summary: Two individuals reportedly from the CIA died in a car crash in Chihuahua, Mexico, prompting Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum to order an investigation into whether foreign agents were operating in the country without federal authorization. Mexican state authorities have disputed reports of CIA operational involvement, claiming the Americans were instructors being driven to an airport, while the Washington Post cited anonymous sources saying they were on a counternarcotics mission. The incident heightens ongoing tensions between Mexico and the United States over potential unilateral US security operations on Mexican soil.

claims.claim: Two individuals reportedly from the CIA were killed in a car crash in the Mexican state of Chihuahua early on Sunday.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum ordered the Attorney General's Office to investigate whether Mexican constitutional or national security law was violated.
Mexican law requires foreign agents to obtain federal authorization before operating in the country and prohibits them from working directly with state-level officials without prior approval.
The Washington Post, citing anonymous officials, reported the two agents were engaged in a counternarcotics operation.
Chihuahua state Attorney General Cesar Jauregui Moreno ruled out the intervention of foreign elements in the drug operation, saying the Americans were being given a lift to the airport.
US Ambassador Ronald Johnson referred to the deceased as 'embassy personnel' and expressed condolences on social media.

Trump has privately described cartels as 'unlawful combatants' in an 'armed conflict' with the US in a notice to Congress, and has carried out bombings of alleged drug-smuggling boats killing at least 180 people.
The contradictory statements from Mexican federal and state authorities suggest confusion or deliberate obfuscation about the nature of the US officials' presence in Chihuahua.
The incident reinforces broader tensions over whether the Trump administration may pursue unilateral ground operations in Mexico in violation of its sovereignty.
Legal experts have described Trump's January attack on Venezuela, which resulted in Maduro's abduction, as a violation of international law.

articles.title: Mexican president in the dark about drug operation after which US, Mexican officials were killed
bullets: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum stated her government was not informed about the drug operation in Chihuahua during which two U.S. embassy personnel and two Mexican officials were killed in a car accident.

The four individuals died when their vehicle went off a road into a ravine in the early hours of Sunday morning while traveling in a convoy returning from a drug lab operation in the municipality of Morelos.

Chihuahua state attorney general César Jáuregui Moreno clarified that U.S. personnel were not part of the operation itself but were drone-training instructors who joined the convoy for a ride after completing a separate course in Chihuahua city.
U.S. Ambassador Ronald Johnson expressed condolences on X and said the tragedy 'strengthens our resolve to continue their mission and advance our shared commitment to security and justice.'
articles.summary: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said Monday she was not informed about a joint drug lab operation in Chihuahua state during which two U.S. embassy personnel and two Mexican officials died in a vehicle accident. Chihuahua state attorney general César Jáuregui Moreno stated that U.S. personnel were drone-training instructors who joined a convoy returning from the operation rather than participants in the raid itself. Sheinbaum indicated her government would investigate whether proper constitutional protocols governing foreign collaboration were followed.

claims.claim: Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said her government's security cabinet was not informed about the Chihuahua drug operation.
Two U.S. embassy personnel and two members of the Chihuahua State Investigation Agency (AEI) were killed when their vehicle went off a road into a ravine.
The operation targeted clandestine drug laboratories in the municipality of Morelos in the Sierra de Chihuahua.
Chihuahua attorney general César Jáuregui Moreno stated that approximately 80 officers participated in the drug lab seizure, 40 from the AEI and 40 from the Secretariat of National Defense.
Jáuregui Moreno stated the U.S. personnel were drone-operation instructors who had been giving a course in Polanco and joined the convoy for transport, not as participants in the operation.
U.S. Ambassador Ronald Johnson posted condolences on X and called the incident 'a solemn reminder of the risks faced by those Mexican and U.S. officials dedicated to protecting our communities.'
Sheinbaum indicated state governments must obtain authorization from Mexico's federal government before collaborating with U.S. or other foreign entities, as required by the Constitution.
The discrepancy between the attorney general's account of U.S. personnel as non-participants and their presence in the convoy returning from the operation suggests ambiguity about their actual role.
The U.S. State Department declined to provide further comment to Fox News Digital.

headlines.title: UPS, FedEx, DHL File for IEEPA Tariff Refunds After Supreme Court Ruling
headlines.framing: UPS, FedEx, and DHL filed for refunds through a new CBP portal after the Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs in February.
reported: BBC News: 56,000+ importers applied for $127bn in IEEPA tariff refunds.
CBS News: UPS, FedEx, DHL pledged to pass refunds to original payers.
CBS News: Trump warned he'd 'remember' companies not filing CAPE claims.
BBC News: Class-action suits filed against FedEx, Costco, EssilorLuxottica.

articles.title: UPS, FedEx and DHL file for tariff refunds. What it means for consumers.
bullets: UPS, FedEx, and DHL have filed claims through the CBP's newly launched CAPE portal seeking refunds for IEEPA tariffs ruled illegally imposed by the Supreme Court in February.
All three companies pledged to pass refunds on to customers who originally bore the tariff costs, with CBP expected to deliver refunds within 60 to 90 days of claim approval.
Only the importer of record can file through the CAPE portal, meaning individual consumers and small businesses must wait for shippers to receive and redistribute funds.
President Trump suggested he would 'remember' U.S. companies that do not submit refund claims, calling it 'brilliant' if firms opted not to seek reimbursement from the federal government.
articles.summary: UPS, FedEx, and DHL have filed for tariff refunds through a new U.S. Customs and Border Protection portal after the Supreme Court ruled in February that IEEPA-based tariffs were illegally imposed. The three major shippers have committed to redistributing any approved refunds to customers who originally paid the levies, though CBP is expected to take 60 to 90 days to process approved claims. President Trump separately indicated he would favor companies that forgo refund claims, calling it 'brilliant' for firms to decline to seek reimbursement.

claims.claim: UPS, FedEx, and DHL filed for tariff refunds through CBP's CAPE portal after it launched on Monday.
The Supreme Court ruled in February that IEEPA tariffs had been illegally imposed.
CBP said it will deliver refunds to successful applicants within 60 to 90 days of claim approval.
Only the importer of record can request refunds through the CAPE portal; individual consumers cannot file claims directly.
UPS stated it will issue refunds to customers after receiving funds from CBP, with no action required from those customers.
FedEx said its intent is to issue refunds for IEEPA tariffs paid to shippers and consumers who originally bore the charges.
President Trump told CNBC he will 'remember' U.S. companies that do not submit CAPE declarations.
Trump's suggestion that he would favor firms declining refunds represents an unusual use of presidential influence to discourage companies from exercising a legal right.
Small businesses like Houghton Horns face uncertainty because they must rely on shippers to process and pass along refunds without guaranteed timelines.
The shippers did not disclose the total amounts of refunds they are seeking.

articles.title: Trump tariff refunds begin but consumers likely to miss out
bullets: The Trump administration launched the Cape portal on Monday to process refunds for more than $160bn in tariffs struck down by the US Supreme Court in February.
As of early April, over 56,000 importers had completed steps to apply for refunds totaling $127bn, with successful applicants expected to receive payment within 60 to 90 days.

Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in February it was 'unlikely' consumers would benefit from the refunds, while US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer urged companies to give any windfall to workers as bonuses instead.
Class-action lawsuits have been filed against EssilorLuxottica, FedEx, and Costco by individuals arguing that tariff refunds received by businesses should be passed on to consumers.
articles.summary: The US government has opened an online portal to begin processing refunds for over $160bn in tariffs collected under the IEEPA after the Supreme Court struck them down in February. While more than 56,000 importers have applied for refunds worth $127bn, senior Trump administration officials have indicated they do not expect consumers—who bore higher prices—to see direct relief. Early reports from small businesses describe a mixed experience with the new system, and class-action lawsuits have been filed against several major retailers seeking to compel the pass-through of any refunds.

claims.claim: The US Supreme Court struck down Trump's IEEPA emergency tariffs in February.
The US Court of International Trade ordered customs officials in March to refund more than $160bn collected under IEEPA duties.
More than 56,000 importers had completed the necessary application steps by early April, with claims worth $127bn.
The Cape portal went live on Monday and processes refunds as a lump sum rather than item by item.
Successful refund applicants can expect payment, plus applicable interest, within 60 to 90 days.
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent suggested in February that it was unlikely consumers would benefit from the tariff refunds.
Class-action lawsuits have been filed against EssilorLuxottica, FedEx, and Costco alleging businesses should pass refunds on to consumers.
The refund process is unlikely to compensate most consumers directly, as businesses have no legal obligation to disburse funds to those who paid higher prices.
Future tariffs proposed by the Trump administration could wipe out any refunds received by importers, according to We Pay the Tariffs executive director Dan Anthony.
The tariff burden on businesses included harder-to-quantify costs such as lost sales and debt taken on to pay duties, complicating full restitution even for importers.

headlines.title: Gunman Kills Canadian Tourist at Mexico's Teotihuacan Pyramids, Injuring Thirteen
headlines.framing: A lone gunman shot and killed a Canadian woman at Mexico's Teotihuacan pyramids on April 20, injuring 13 others before dying by suicide.
established: A gunman opened fire at the Pyramid of the Moon at Teotihuacan on April 20, killing a Canadian woman and injuring at least 13 people of multiple nationalities before dying from a self-inflicted gunshot wound.
The attacker, identified as Julio César Jasso Ramírez, 27, a Mexican national, was confirmed by authorities to have acted alone.
Authorities recovered a firearm, a bladed weapon, and ammunition at the scene; Jasso's backpack also contained materials referencing the 1999 Columbine High School massacre.
Seven of the injured sustained gunshot wounds; others were hurt in falls while fleeing, with victims ranging in age from 6 to 61.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum expressed condolences, pledged a thorough investigation, and confirmed contact with the Canadian Embassy.
The Teotihuacan site, which drew over 1.8 million international visitors last year, was closed following the attack, raising security concerns ahead of Mexico's co-hosting of the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
reported: Fox News: Video showed Jasso threatening to sacrifice tourists, claiming two Korean deaths.
BBC: Witness said gunman fired mostly into air, carried digital tablet.
NBC News: Broader site to reopen Wednesday; Pyramid of Moon stays closed.
NPR, Fox News: Local guide said routine entry security screenings discontinued.

disputed.claim: Number of American nationals among those injured

positions.asserts: Seven Americans were among those injured.
Six Americans were among those hospitalized.

articles.title: Gunman shoots several tourists at historic pyramids in Mexico
bullets: A gunman opened fire on tourists at the Teotihuacan pyramids in Mexico on Monday, killing one Canadian and injuring at least 13 others before dying from a self-inflicted gunshot wound.
The attacker was identified as 27-year-old Julio Cesar Jasso, a Mexican national who fired from the top of the Pyramid of the Moon; authorities confirmed he was the sole assailant.
Those hospitalized included six Americans, three Colombians, two Brazilians, one Russian, and one Canadian, with victims ranging in age from 6 to 61 years old.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said the shooting would be investigated and that she was in contact with the Canadian Embassy; the Teotihuacan site was closed until further notice.
articles.summary: A gunman opened fire on tourists at the UNESCO World Heritage Site of Teotihuacan, north of Mexico City, on Monday, killing one Canadian and wounding at least 13 others before dying of a self-inflicted gunshot wound, Mexican authorities said. The attacker, identified as 27-year-old Julio Cesar Jasso, fired from atop the Pyramid of the Moon, sending hundreds of visitors fleeing in panic. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum expressed condolences and pledged an investigation, while the archaeological site was closed indefinitely.

claims.claim: A gunman opened fire on tourists at the Teotihuacan pyramids on Monday, killing one Canadian and injuring at least 13 people.
The shooter was identified as 27-year-old Julio Cesar Jasso of Mexico, according to a state official speaking anonymously to the AP.
Jasso died from a self-inflicted gunshot wound; authorities found a gun, a knife, and ammunition on him.
The State of Mexico government confirmed Jasso was the sole assailant in the attack.
Those hospitalized included six Americans, three Colombians, two Brazilians, one Russian, and one Canadian, ranging in age from 6 to 61.
The Teotihuacan archaeological site drew more than 1.8 million international visitors last year, according to government figures.
Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum said the shooting would be investigated and that she was in contact with the Canadian Embassy.
Security scans at the entrance of the archaeological site, once carried out by staff, have since been discontinued, according to a local guide.
The lapse in security screening may have allowed the attacker to bring a weapon onto the pyramid grounds undetected.
The National Institute of Anthropology and History announced the Teotihuacan site will remain closed until further notice.

articles.title: Survivors of Teotihuacan pyramid shooting recount daring escape
bullets: A gunman identified as 27-year-old Julián César Jasso Ramírez opened fire atop the Pyramid of the Moon at Teotihuacán on Monday, killing one Canadian woman and injuring at least 13 foreign visitors before shooting himself dead as police closed in.

Mexican authorities described Jasso as a copycat killer influenced by foreign school shootings, including the 1999 Columbine massacre, and said his backpack contained literature about other mass shootings.

Survivors Jalen Aybar and Jaslim Landaverde, a couple from Chicago, escaped by jumping down a series of pyramid platforms, with Landaverde breaking her foot in the process during the approximately 25-minute siege.

Criminologist Adam Lankford of the University of Alabama said his data show a global rise in mass shootings influenced by U.S. attacks, noting that 'attacking a national landmark fits the idea of a fame-seeking attack.'
articles.summary: A gunman opened fire on tourists at the ancient Mesoamerican site of Teotihuacán near Mexico City on Monday, killing a Canadian woman and wounding at least 13 others before dying by a self-inflicted gunshot wound as authorities arrived. Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum described the attacker as a copycat influenced by foreign mass shootings, including Columbine, and pledged to strengthen security at archaeological sites. The incident has raised concerns about Mexico's tourist industry ahead of its hosting of World Cup matches beginning in June.

claims.claim: Julián César Jasso Ramírez, 27, opened fire atop the Pyramid of the Moon at Teotihuacán on Monday, killing one Canadian woman and injuring at least 13 foreign visitors.
The gunman shot himself dead as police and National Guard troops climbed the pyramid to confront him, approximately 25 minutes after the attack began.
Mexican authorities said the attacker's backpack contained literature about other mass shootings, including the 1999 Columbine High School massacre.
President Claudia Sheinbaum said the attacker 'had traits of psychological problems and was influenced by episodes that have occurred elsewhere.'
All those killed or injured were foreign visitors; it was unclear whether the shooter deliberately targeted foreigners.
Criminologist Adam Lankford said his data show a global rise in mass shootings, with many shooters influenced by killings in the United States.

articles.title: The Onion launches new bid to take over Alex Jones' Infowars and turn it into a parody platform
bullets: The Onion has submitted a new proposal to a Texas state judge seeking an exclusive, temporary six-month license to the intellectual property of Infowars' parent company, Free Speech Systems, to operate Infowars as a parody platform.

Under the proposal, The Onion would pay $81,000 a month in rent and operating costs, with profits directed to Sandy Hook families who hold more than $1.4 billion in defamation judgments against Alex Jones.

The Onion CEO Ben Collins stated the outlet has already hired comedian Tim Heidecker to help run the parody site, with a target launch date of around April 30 pending Judge Maya Guerra Gamble's approval.

Jones vowed to fight the licensing proposal in court but acknowledged he could be removed from the Infowars building by month's end, saying he would continue his show under the 'Alex Jones Show' name on his personal X account and other platforms.
articles.summary: The Onion has filed a new proposal with a Texas state court to obtain a temporary license to operate Infowars as a parody platform, following Alex Jones' company's failure to satisfy over $1.4 billion in defamation judgments owed to Sandy Hook shooting victims' families. The proposal, submitted Monday, would have The Onion pay $81,000 monthly in operating costs while directing profits to the families, with a potential start date of April 30. Jones said he would contest the plan legally but acknowledged he may be forced out of the Infowars building, pledging to continue his show independently.

claims.claim: The Onion submitted a proposal Monday to a Texas state judge seeking an exclusive, temporary license to Infowars' intellectual property to operate it as a parody platform.

headlines.title: Roxanne Khamsi Book Argues Somatic Mutations Continuously Shape Human Health
headlines.framing: Science writer Roxanne Khamsi's 'Beyond Inheritance' presents research on how lifelong somatic mutations influence health, aging, and healing.
reported: Science News: Khamsi's 'Beyond Inheritance' published by Riverhead Books at $30.

articles.title: ‘Beyond Inheritance’ offers a new view on mutations
bullets: Science writer Roxanne Khamsi's book 'Beyond Inheritance' argues that somatic mutations occurring throughout life—not just inherited ones—continuously shape human health and lifespan.
Khamsi describes cases such as tyrosinemia where diseased liver cells spontaneously mutate back toward a healthy state, suggesting mutations can sometimes be a force for healing rather than harm.
The book contends that accumulated somatic mutations may be a key driver of aging, and notes that companies are already attempting to halt aging by eliminating highly mutated cells.

Khamsi concludes that humans should selectively embrace beneficial or harmless mutations rather than seek to eliminate all mutation, cautioning that precision and wisdom must guide genetic intervention.
articles.summary: Science writer Roxanne Khamsi's 'Beyond Inheritance' (Riverhead Books, $30) presents a broad-audience synthesis of current research on somatic mutations, arguing they are a constant, life-shaping biological force rather than events confined to inheritance or disease. The book highlights cases in which spontaneous mutations correct genetic defects and explores the connection between mutation accumulation and aging. Science News reviews the work favorably, describing Khamsi's writing as accessible and well-researched.

claims.claim: Roxanne Khamsi's book 'Beyond Inheritance' is published by Riverhead Books at a price of $30.
The human body contains approximately 30 trillion to 40 trillion human cells, each accumulating its own DNA changes over time.
Khamsi argues that genetic destinies are not necessarily defined by what individuals inherit from their biological parents.
In tyrosinemia, a condition in which a protein building block accumulates in the liver, some affected cells have been observed mutating back to a healthy state.
Companies are currently attempting to slow or halt aging by targeting and eliminating highly mutated cells.
Accumulated somatic mutations may be a fundamental mechanism underlying aging and the limits of human lifespan.
Understanding the mechanisms of beneficial spontaneous mutations could lead to new therapeutic approaches for genetic disorders like tyrosinemia.
Humans should selectively welcome helpful mutations and accept harmless ones rather than pursuing blanket elimination of all mutation.
Intervening to stop all mutations without sufficient precision could be unwise, even if technically possible.
Science is only at the beginning of understanding how dynamic living cells can be in terms of ongoing genetic change.

headlines.title: Scattered Spider Senior Member Tyler Buchanan Pleads Guilty to Wire Fraud
