articles.title: ‘Scattered Spider’ Member ‘Tylerb’ Pleads Guilty
bullets: Tyler Robert Buchanan, a 24-year-old British national and senior Scattered Spider member known as 'Tylerb,' has pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft.

Buchanan admitted to orchestrating tens of thousands of SMS-based phishing attacks in 2022 targeting companies including Twilio, LastPass, DoorDash, and Mailchimp, then using stolen data to conduct SIM-swapping attacks that stole at least $8 million in cryptocurrency from individual victims.

Buchanan was arrested by Spanish authorities in June 2024 while attempting to board a flight to Italy and was extradited to the United States, where he has been held in federal custody since April 2025.

He faces a statutory maximum sentence of 22 years in federal prison, with his sentencing hearing scheduled for August 21, 2026; a co-conspirator, Noah Michael Urban, was previously sentenced to 10 years and ordered to pay $13 million in restitution.
articles.summary: Tyler Robert Buchanan, a 24-year-old Scottish national and senior member of the cybercrime group Scattered Spider, pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft for his role in a 2022 SMS phishing campaign that breached multiple major technology companies and enabled cryptocurrency theft. Buchanan, whose hacker alias 'Tylerb' appeared on a criminal leaderboard, admitted to stealing at least $8 million in virtual currency from U.S. victims via SIM-swapping attacks. He faces up to 22 years in federal prison and is scheduled to be sentenced on August 21, 2026.

claims.claim: Tyler Robert Buchanan, 24, a British national, pleaded guilty to wire fraud conspiracy and aggravated identity theft.
Buchanan and Scattered Spider co-conspirators launched tens of thousands of SMS phishing attacks in 2022, breaching Twilio, LastPass, DoorDash, and Mailchimp.
The U.S. Justice Department stated Buchanan admitted to stealing at least $8 million in virtual currency from individual U.S. victims.
FBI investigators linked Buchanan to the phishing campaign via shared usernames, email addresses, and IP address records provided by NameCheap and Scottish police.
Buchanan was arrested by Spanish authorities in June 2024 while attempting to board a flight to Italy and was extradited to the United States.
Noah Michael Urban, a previously convicted Scattered Spider member, was sentenced to 10 years in federal prison and ordered to pay $13 million in restitution.
Scattered Spider is connected to the broader 'The Com' cybercriminal community, which coordinates via Telegram and Discord and routinely uses social engineering as an attack vector.
Buchanan's ultimate sentence is likely to be significantly lower than the 22-year statutory maximum due to mitigating factors under U.S. Sentencing Guidelines.

Two alleged Scattered Spider members, Owen Flowers and Thalha Jubair, are awaiting trial in the United Kingdom in June for hacking and extorting U.K. retailers, London transit, and U.S. healthcare providers.
A rival cybercrime gang hired assailants to attack Buchanan's mother and threaten him with a blowtorch to obtain his cryptocurrency wallet keys, prompting him to flee the U.K. in February 2023.

articles.title: Scottish man pleads guilty to attack spree that created Scattered Spider’s notoriety
bullets: Tyler Robert Buchanan, 24, of Dundee, Scotland, pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit wire fraud and aggravated identity theft for his role as a core leader of Scattered Spider, the Justice Department announced Friday.

Buchanan and his co-conspirators stole more than $8 million in cryptocurrency from U.S. residents via phishing and SIM-swapping attacks between September 2021 and April 2023, targeting at least a dozen companies across entertainment, telecom, and technology sectors.
Buchanan faces up to 22 years in federal prison at his August 21 sentencing; he was arrested by Spanish police in Palma in June 2024 as he attempted to board a charter flight to Naples, Italy.

Unit 221B chief research officer Allison Nixon described Buchanan as "the glue that held this gang together," and noted that Spanish police found bitcoin worth more than $27 million in his control at the time of arrest.
articles.summary: Tyler Robert Buchanan, a Scottish national identified as a core leader of Scattered Spider, pleaded guilty Friday to federal wire fraud and identity theft charges stemming from a phishing and SIM-swapping campaign that stole over $8 million in cryptocurrency from U.S. victims between 2021 and 2023. Buchanan, arrested in Spain in June 2024 while attempting to travel internationally, faces up to 22 years in prison at his August sentencing. Three alleged co-conspirators — Ahmed Hossam Eldin Elbadawy, Evans Onyeaka Osiebo, and Joel Martin Evans — still face charges in the case.

claims.claim: Tyler Robert Buchanan of Dundee, Scotland pleaded guilty to conspiracy to commit wire fraud and aggravated identity theft.

Buchanan's international travel made him legally vulnerable to arrest, as most countries are willing to support the arrest of foreign criminals, according to Nixon.
A rival Com gang allegedly broke into Buchanan's home and used a blowtorch on him to extract cryptocurrency keys before his arrest.

The Com has grown to thousands of members, typically between 11 and 25 years old, and has splintered into three primary subsets described by the FBI as Hacker Com, In Real Life Com, and Extortion Com.

bullets: Ofcom, the UK's communications regulator, has launched a formal investigation into Telegram under the Online Safety Act over alleged failure to prevent child sexual abuse material (CSAM) from being shared on its platform.
Evidence prompting the investigation came from the Canadian Centre for Child Protection as well as Ofcom's own platform assessment.

Telegram denied the allegations, stating it 'virtually eliminated the public spread of CSAM' since 2018 and suggested the probe may be 'part of a broader attack on online platforms that defend freedom of speech and the right to privacy.'
Ofcom can impose fines of up to £18 million or 10% of qualifying worldwide revenue, and in serious cases can seek court orders to effectively ban non-compliant platforms in the UK.

headlines.title: Brazilian Researchers Use Compressed-Air Guns to Remove Invasive Sun Corals

articles.title: Got pesky, invasive corals? Blast ‘em away with air guns
bullets: Researchers in Brazil tested compressed-air guns to remove invasive sun corals (Tubastraea) from the Alcatrazes Archipelago Wildlife Refuge, blasting 48 colonies and leaving 14 untouched as controls.
The air-gun method largely obliterated targeted colonies, and lab samples collected during removal failed to regenerate, addressing concerns that tissue fragments could re-colonize.

The study, published April 4 in Ecological Solutions and Evidence, was led by Guilherme Pereira-Filho of the Federal University of São Paulo, who described the method as 'quite simple' and relatively inexpensive.
Researchers plan to scale up the technique to island-wide applications and develop equipment capable of removing sun corals from ship hulls, a major vector for the species' spread.
articles.summary: Brazilian researchers have developed a compressed-air gun method for removing invasive sun corals, reporting in Ecological Solutions and Evidence that blasting colonies in a wildlife refuge effectively destroyed them without generating regenerative tissue fragments. The technique, which adapts lab practices for underwater use, is presented as a cheaper and more thorough alternative to pneumatic hammering. The team is now planning larger-scale trials and equipment capable of targeting coral-encrusted ship hulls.

claims.claim: Invasive sun corals (Tubastraea) first arrived in Brazil in the 1980s, though their precise origin is unclear.
The most common current removal method for invasive corals is pneumatic hammering, which often releases fragments that can form new colonies.
Pereira-Filho and colleagues connected an air gun to a diving regulator and blasted 48 sun coral colonies in Brazil's Alcatrazes Archipelago Wildlife Refuge.
None of the tissue samples collected during air-gun removal regenerated in lab aquariums, suggesting the method does not create viable propagules.
The study was published on April 4 in the journal Ecological Solutions and Evidence.
Marine ecologist Joel Creed of Rio de Janeiro State University called the experimental procedures 'very well thought out.'
Ship hulls are described as a significant source of invasive coral spread.
The compressed-air method is more effective than pneumatic hammering at reaching underwater crevices where sun corals grow.
Compressed-air blasting is a cost-effective and scalable improvement over existing invasive coral removal techniques.
Allowing invasive sun corals to remain unchecked would result in hundreds of larvae produced per year, according to Creed.

headlines.title: Book Chronicles Lean Proof Assistant Rise as AI Math Training Tool
headlines.framing: Science News reviews Kevin Hartnett's book on the Lean proof assistant and its role in AI mathematical reasoning.
reported: Science News: AlphaProof scored 28/42 at 2024 IMO, reaching silver-medal level.
Science News: Leo de Moura launched Lean at Microsoft Research in 2013.
Science News: By 2025, tens of thousands of users ran projects on Lean.

articles.title: The Proof in the Code traces efforts to digitally verify mathematical truths
bullets: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof scored 28 out of 42 points at the 2024 International Mathematical Olympiad, reaching the silver-medal level as an unofficial participant.

The proof assistant program Lean, launched by Leo de Moura at Microsoft Research in 2013 as a software code-checking tool, has become central to both mathematical research and AI training for mathematical reasoning.

By 2025, tens of thousands of users across academia and technology were running ambitious projects on Lean, including AI researchers who used its extensive math libraries to train models like AlphaProof.

Kevin Hartnett's book 'The Proof in the Code' chronicles the collaborative effort of mathematicians and developers to expand Lean's libraries and usability, including Kevin Buzzard's months-long project to encode perfectoid spaces.
articles.summary: Science News reviews 'The Proof in the Code' by Kevin Hartnett, a book tracing the development of the proof assistant program Lean from a Microsoft Research software tool into a foundational resource for mathematical verification and AI training. The review describes how a small community of mathematicians painstakingly built up Lean's libraries, enabling AI systems like Google DeepMind's AlphaProof to achieve silver-medal-level performance at the 2024 International Mathematical Olympiad. The reviewer finds the book an inspiring account of collaboration, though notes its non-linear structure can occasionally confuse readers.

claims.claim: Google DeepMind's AlphaProof scored 28 out of 42 points at the 2024 International Mathematical Olympiad as an unofficial participant, reaching the silver-medal cutoff.
Leo de Moura launched Lean in 2013 as a software code-checking tool while working as a software engineer at Microsoft Research.
Jeremy Avigad of Carnegie Mellon University began using Lean to write math proofs almost immediately after the program's launch.
Kevin Buzzard and collaborators spent months translating perfectoid spaces into Lean in 2018, producing many thousands of lines of code.
By 2025, tens of thousands of users across academia and technology were using Lean for increasingly ambitious projects, according to Hartnett's book.
Proof assistant programs can verify human-written mathematical proofs but cannot independently generate new proofs.
Training AI systems to automate mathematical reasoning was considered a grand challenge as recently as 2020.
Lean's extensive math libraries became a key resource for training math-solving AI models, connecting the proof assistant community to AI research.
The book's non-linear structure, while occasionally confusing, adds engrossing texture to the history of Lean for mathematically curious readers.

The $225 million penalty was agreed to in a 2020 pact to resolve federal civil and criminal investigations against the OxyContin maker.
If the judge approves the forfeiture, other penalties will not be collected in exchange for Purdue settling thousands of additional opioid-related lawsuits.
Under the broader settlement, the Sackler family will make payments to state, local, and Native American tribal governments, individual victims, and others.
articles.summary: A federal judge is set to approve a $225 million forfeiture by Purdue Pharma to the US Department of Justice, stemming from a 2020 agreement to resolve federal civil and criminal investigations into the company's role in the opioid crisis. articles.title: Purdue Pharma to be sentenced in opioids case, allowing settlement money to flow
bullets: A judge is expected to sentence Purdue Pharma to forfeit $225 million to the U.S. Justice Department on Tuesday, clearing the way for a broader opioid settlement to take effect May 1.

The broader settlement requires Sackler family members who own the company to pay up to $7 billion over 15 years to state, local, and Native American tribal governments, some individual victims, and others.

Purdue pleaded guilty in November 2020 to three federal criminal charges, including admitting it paid doctors to prescribe opioids and failed to prevent its painkillers from being diverted to the black market.

Some victims and family members, including Susan Ousterman whose son died at 24 from an overdose, are urging the judge to reject the plea deal and pursue criminal charges against individuals including Sackler family members.
articles.summary: A federal judge is set to sentence Purdue Pharma to forfeit $225 million to the Justice Department on Tuesday, finalizing a 2020 criminal plea deal and allowing a broader settlement of thousands of opioid lawsuits to take effect. The settlement requires Sackler family members to contribute up to $7 billion over 15 years, while Purdue itself will cease to exist and be replaced by a new public-benefit company called Knoa Pharma. Some victims and survivors oppose the deal, arguing it falls short of justice and that no family members have faced criminal charges despite the opioid crisis being linked to roughly 900,000 deaths in the U.S. since 1999.

claims.claim: A judge is expected to sentence Purdue Pharma to forfeit $225 million to the U.S. Justice Department on Tuesday.
Purdue pleaded guilty to three federal criminal charges in November 2020.
The broader settlement requires Sackler family members to pay up to $7 billion over 15 years to government entities, individual victims, and others.
The government agreed in the plea deal not to collect $5.3 billion in criminal forfeitures and fines and $2.8 billion in civil liabilities.
Sackler family members received $10.7 billion in payments from Purdue between 2008 and 2018.
Purdue will cease to exist and be replaced by a new company, Knoa Pharma, operating for the public benefit with a board appointed by the states.
By the end of last year, Purdue had paid law firms and other professionals more than $1 billion in fees related to the case.

articles.title: CISA flags new SD-WAN flaw as actively exploited in attacks
bullets: CISA added CVE-2026-20133, an information disclosure flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog based on evidence of active exploitation.

Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies were given until Friday, April 24 to patch their systems, with CISA referencing Emergency Directive 26-03 and its Hunt & Hardening Guidance for Cisco SD-WAN Devices.

Cisco patched CVE-2026-20133 in late February, describing it as allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to read sensitive information on the underlying operating system via API access due to insufficient file system restrictions.
Cisco's own PSIRT advisory still states it is 'not aware of any public announcements or malicious use' of CVE-2026-20133, creating a discrepancy with CISA's active-exploitation designation.
articles.summary: CISA has added a Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager information disclosure vulnerability (CVE-2026-20133) to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered federal agencies to patch by April 24. Cisco had patched the flaw in late February, noting it allows unauthenticated remote attackers to access sensitive system information via API. Notably, Cisco's own security advisory has not yet confirmed active exploitation, contrasting with CISA's assessment.

claims.claim: CISA added CVE-2026-20133 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on Monday, April 21, 2026, citing evidence of active exploitation.

Cisco's PSIRT stated it is 'not aware of any public announcements or malicious use' of CVE-2026-20133 as of the time of publication.
One week after the February patches, Cisco confirmed two other vulnerabilities patched the same day (CVE-2026-20128 and CVE-2026-20122) were being actively exploited in the wild.
In February, Cisco tagged CVE-2026-20127, a critical authentication bypass, as exploited in zero-day attacks enabling threat actors to add rogue peers to networks since at least 2023.
CISA has tagged 91 Cisco vulnerabilities as exploited in the wild over the last several years, six of which have been used by ransomware operations.

bullets: CISA added eight new vulnerabilities to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, including three flaws in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, citing evidence of active exploitation.

CVE-2025-32975 in Quest KACE Systems Management Appliance carries a maximum CVSS score of 10.0 and was observed being weaponized by unknown threat actors against unpatched systems as recently as last month, according to Arctic Wolf.

articles.title: Uber found liable for actions of driver who grabbed passenger's inner thigh
bullets: A federal jury in Charlotte, North Carolina found Uber liable for a driver who grabbed a passenger's inner thigh and awarded the plaintiff $5,000 in damages.
The case is a bellwether trial within a broader group of sexual assault lawsuits filed against Uber across multiple jurisdictions, and is the third such case to go to trial.

Judge Charles R. Breyer ruled Uber is a 'common carrier' under North Carolina law, making it liable for driver misconduct despite Uber's long-standing position that its gig-worker drivers are independent contractors.
Uber stated the small financial judgment supports its view that these cases have been overstated and said it has strong grounds for appeal, arguing the jury was incorrectly instructed on liability.
articles.summary: A federal jury in Charlotte, North Carolina found Uber liable for a driver's sexual battery against a passenger and awarded $5,000 in damages, in a bellwether case from a broader set of sexual assault lawsuits pending against the company. The ruling hinged on Judge Charles R. Breyer's earlier determination that Uber qualifies as a 'common carrier' under North Carolina law, negating the platform's standard defense that its drivers are independent contractors rather than employees. Uber called the small award a vindication of its position and said it plans to appeal, while plaintiff's attorneys characterized the verdict as a win given that Uber itself selected the case as a favorable test case.

claims.claim: A federal jury in Charlotte, North Carolina found Uber liable for a driver who grabbed a passenger's inner thigh and awarded $5,000 in damages.
The North Carolina case is the third bellwether sexual assault lawsuit against Uber to go to trial.
In February, a federal jury in Arizona ordered Uber to pay $8.5 million to a woman who said one of its drivers raped her during a trip.
A California jury found Uber not liable for the alleged assault of a rider last year.
Judge Charles R. Breyer ruled Uber is a 'common carrier' under North Carolina law and therefore liable for driver misconduct.
Uber stated it has strong grounds for appeal because it believes the jury was incorrectly instructed on the question of liability.
Uber's classification of drivers as independent contractors has long formed the basis for its argument that it is not liable for driver misconduct.
Plaintiff's attorney Ellyn Hurd argued the verdict is favorable because Uber itself selected the North Carolina case as a test case it expected to win.
The verdict in a case Uber chose as favorable to itself bodes well for the remaining plaintiffs in the broader lawsuit group.
Florida and Texas have explicitly exempted rideshare providers from common carrier liability, while North Carolina has not.

bullets: A federal jury in Charlotte, North Carolina found Uber liable for a driver who grabbed a passenger's inner thigh and asked if he could 'keep her,' awarding $5,000 in damages.

Uber said it has strong grounds for appeal due to allegedly incorrect jury instructions on liability, and noted the $5,000 award 'represents a tiny fraction of previous demands.'

headlines.title: Cameroon Physics School Connects Central African Researchers Despite Infrastructure Challenges
headlines.framing: The seventh Central African School on Electronic Structure Methods and Applications was held at the University of Dschang, Cameroon, as a satellite to the 2025 Global Physics Summit.

articles.title: Shining a light on central African physics
bullets: The seventh Central African School on Electronic Structure Methods and Applications was held at the University of Dschang in Cameroon as one of 23 satellite events to the Global Physics Summit (GPS) organized by the American Physical Society (APS) in Denver.

The Purpose-led Publishing (PLP) coalition — comprising APS, AIP Publishing, and IOP Publishing — hosted satellite events across Africa, Asia, the Middle East, and South America to broaden participation in the GPS, which drew 14,000 delegates.

Participants travelled significant distances to attend, including Cladi Rodnet Boulingui, who spent three days on a bus from Brazzaville, Republic of Congo, with sponsorship from the Universität Duisburg-Essen in Germany.
The event faced infrastructure challenges including power outages, lack of WiFi, and no running water at the university, with organizers providing their own routers and generators to run sessions.
articles.summary: Physics World reporter Victoria Merriman attended the seventh Central African School on Electronic Structure Methods and Applications at the University of Dschang, Cameroon, held as a satellite to the 2025 Global Physics Summit. The event, co-organized by the Purpose-led Publishing coalition, brought together researchers from across central Africa despite significant infrastructure constraints including frequent power outages and absent internet connectivity. Organizer Stephane Kenmoe, an associate professor at Universität Duisburg-Essen, led efforts to connect the region's physics community to global networks and resources.

claims.claim: The seventh Central African School on Electronic Structure Methods and Applications was held at the University of Dschang in Cameroon.
The Global Physics Summit in Denver is described as the world's biggest physics conference, with 14,000 delegates.
APS, AIP Publishing, and IOP Publishing form the Purpose-led Publishing (PLP) coalition, which organized 23 satellite events globally.
Cladi Rodnet Boulingui travelled three days by bus from Brazzaville, Republic of Congo, to attend the school.
Stephane Kenmoe is an associate professor at the faculty of chemistry at Universität Duisburg-Essen and the organizing director of the school.
The University of Dschang lacked WiFi, running water for toilets, and reliable power during the event.
The satellite event format is an effective mechanism for extending global physics conference participation to researchers who cannot obtain visas, funding, or time to travel to Denver.
Kenmoe co-produced a film titled Seeds of Science with the local film industry, addressing child labour and child marriage in the region.
Department heads at Dschang have been called upon to write PhD curricula for neighbouring Francophone countries where scientific funding is lacking.
The author characterizes the passion for learning among participants as persisting despite severe infrastructure challenges.
